page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.se1.xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual server instance belonging to the ecs.se1 family. It is powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor with an x86_64 architecture, operating at a clock speed of 2.5 GHz. The instance features 4 dedicated physical cores with 1 thread per core, providing 4 vCPUs. It is equipped with 32.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. There is no local storage bundled with this instance, and it does not include any GPUs. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Benchmark results indicate that the ecs.se1.xlarge delivers modest performance across compute and memory operations. In stress-ng testing, it achieved a single-core CPU score of 1100.49 and a multi-core score of 2931.93, both placing in the bottom 25% tier. Memory bandwidth tests showed a read-only small block cached speed of 163.82 GB/sec, while large block uncached read and write speeds reached 20.19 GB/sec and 18.14 GB/sec respectively. Database performance, measured via Redis SET operations, reached 2.87 million operations per second. Static web serving throughput was recorded at 2.41 GB/sec.

Given its high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 1:8, this instance is designed for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high-performance compute capabilities. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent performance, though the overall processing power and memory bandwidth are relatively low compared to newer hardware generations. It is suitable for caching layers, small database deployments, and lightweight memory-bound applications.

It features 4 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 8269CY processor running at 2.5 GHz, paired with 32.0 GB of RAM. This configuration provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core, though it lacks local storage and GPU accelerators. Baseline network bandwidth is rated at 1 Gbps. Benchmark data indicates modest performance, with stress-ng multi-core CPU performance at 2931.93 and Redis SET operations at 2.87 million operations per second. The instance is best suited for memory-bound workloads with moderate compute requirements, such as caching layers, small databases, and lightweight application servers.
